question_text: Sound travels at 340 m/s in air but at 1480 m/s in water. Explain why sound travels faster in water than in air, considering what longitudinal compression waves require to propagate.
correct_answer: Water molecules are closer together and more densely packed than air molecules. This allows the compressions and rarefactions of the longitudinal wave to transfer between particles more efficiently and quickly.
explanation: Longitudinal waves need particles close together to transfer the vibrations efficiently. Water is denser (more tightly packed molecules) than air, so compressions pass through faster. It's like passing a message through a crowded room versus a sparse one—denser crowds transfer messages quicker!
